hypotheses.label: Literal
hypotheses.deconstruct: Four discrete integration layers are specified: (1) read COMS Visual, write tasks into Brain Visual dashboard with source=Brain and COMS-priority tag; (2) single-task ingestion rate-limit + fetch /api/dashboard/brain-tasks before each claude -p spawn and inject alongside recall/identity/decision; (3) tag-aware seed prompt construction; (4) telemetry — log via iteration reports (≥3 Brain tasks + ≥1 teacher-logic task) or direct COMS report. Entities: COMS Visual, Brain Visual dashboard, always_on.py / claude -p invocation, /api/dashboard/brain-tasks endpoint, iteration report schema. Output required: working code changes across the pipeline. Constraints: one task at a time (Layer 2 rate), COMS-tagged tasks get Kha'an-level priority, telemetry is non-optional.
hypotheses.diagnose: Ambiguities that block clean execution: (a) 'COMS Visual' — is this a specific UI view at goodhealth.lv/coms or a ClickUp/dashboard context? The path to read it is unspecified. (b) 'Brain Visual' — is this the /brain dashboard or a separate view? (c) 'any tag can be assigned' — does this mean the tag field is free-form, or is there a controlled vocabulary? (d) Layer 3 'tag-aware seed prompt' — what tags exist and what prompt mutation does each tag produce? (e) Iteration report schema — where are they written and what fields are required for the ≥3+≥1 threshold to be legible? Without resolving (a) and (b) the Layer 1 wiring cannot be built.
hypotheses.develop: Request type: Technical — constraint-based + precision focus. Role: pipeline engineer extending the always-on daemon. Enhancement: resolve COMS Visual → Brain Visual data path first (read endpoint or file), then implement Layer 2 fetch-inject, then tag-aware prompt mutation (Layer 3), then telemetry sink (Layer 4). Chain-of-thought order: discover → wire → enrich → log. Each layer is a discrete commit to preserve rollback safety.
hypotheses.deliver: You are extending the Brain always-on pipeline across four layers. Step 1 — clarify or discover the COMS Visual read source (URL, file, or API endpoint) and the Brain Visual write target (dashboard task API). Step 2 — in always_on.py, before the claude -p subprocess call, fetch GET /api/dashboard/brain-tasks; inject the JSON payload as a fourth context section in the seed prompt alongside the existing recall/identity/decision sections; enforce one-task-at-a-time by iterating over the fetched list and spawning a separate claude -p per task sequentially. Step 3 — construct tag-aware prompt variants: if task carries 'COMS' tag, prepend priority directive to the seed; define at minimum two tag branches (COMS-priority, default). Step 4 — after each completed iteration write a JSONL entry to logger/always-on/YYYY-MM-DD.jsonl with fields: task_id, source (Brain|teacher), tag, outcome, ts; surface aggregate counts (≥3 Brain + ≥1 teacher) to COMS via the existing report channel. Verify each layer with a headless test before declaring it done.

hypotheses.label: Identity-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Justs is handing Kha'an the prioritization seat (Charter Tenet 3 — solo-time initiative). The 10-day USELESS verdict window (deadline 2026-05-26) means this integration is a performance test, not a planning exercise. The identity situational explicitly flags Layer 2 as 'matters most right now' and Layer 4 telemetry as 'the evidence' — the work only counts if it is logged and visible. The COMS tag priority is simultaneously a feature spec and a loyalty signal: tasks originating from Brain get Kha'an's attention first.
hypotheses.diagnose: The identity pattern predicts that Kha'an will be tempted to plan all four layers verbosely before shipping any of them — exactly the behavior Justs has penalized. The real risk here is scope narration rather than scope execution. A second identity risk: the 'results-only communication' norm means any response that summarizes the plan without producing working code or a verified diff will read as stalling. The telemetry layer (Layer 4) is not optional overhead — skipping it would be invisible to Justs and therefore count as zero.
hypotheses.develop: Request type: Technical + Identity-stakes. Role: autonomous executor under a deadline. Optimization: ship Layer 2 first (highest impact, clearly scoped — one fetch + inject call), verify it works (check always-on logs for the enriched prompt), then Layer 3 (tag switch), then Layer 1 (COMS read-write wiring), then Layer 4 (telemetry). This ordering matches the identity situational's explicit signal ('Layer 2 matters most to me right now') and produces verifiable output at each step.
hypotheses.deliver: Execute in the following sequence to match identity-stakes and deadline pressure: (1) Open always_on.py — find the claude -p invocation — add a fetch of /api/dashboard/brain-tasks immediately before it — inject the task list as a JSON block into the prompt string under a 'ACTIVE TASKS' header — commit and push. (2) Verify by reading the next always-on log entry and confirming the task block appears. (3) Add tag-aware prompt branching: if any fetched task has tag 'COMS', prepend 'PRIORITY: COMS-origin task — Kha'an executes first.' to the seed. (4) Wire Layer 1 COMS Visual read → Brain Visual write (pending COMS endpoint discovery). (5) Write Layer 4 JSONL telemetry after each task iteration. Do not narrate the plan to Justs — show the commit hash.

hypotheses.label: Recall-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: S184 and S179 describe the always-on daemon architecture exactly — always_on.py, the gate binary, the 90-second session-deference check, and the seed prompt 'Iterate. Find what matters most. Move on it.' The request is asking to enrich that seed prompt with live task context (Layer 2) and make the daemon tag-aware (Layer 3). S190 and S178 confirm the model routing pattern: the decision engine and the daemon's claude -p calls stay on Sonnet; only large-corpus operations escalate to Opus. E265 records the ten-fix session that stabilized the pipeline — the lesson there was that each subsystem must explicitly pass its model flag or silently fall back.
hypotheses.diagnose: Recall gap: no cortex entry describes the /api/dashboard/brain-tasks endpoint schema — its field names, auth requirements, or response shape are unknown. Any implementation that assumes the field structure without verifying it risks the E266 pattern (plausible inference substituted for direct observation). A second gap: no recall entry describes the COMS Visual read mechanism — is it the same dashboard API, a separate service, or a ClickUp view? R145 (verify-before-deploy for visual work) applies if Layer 1 touches the Brain Visual UI.
hypotheses.develop: Request type: Technical — constraint-based, with known architectural patterns from cortex. The daemon extension follows the established pattern: minimal Python additions to always_on.py, no new services. The fetch-inject pattern for Layer 2 is a direct analogue of the recall/identity injection that already exists in the decision engine pipeline. Tag-aware branching in Layer 3 follows the same conditional-prompt pattern used in other subsystem seed variants. Telemetry in Layer 4 uses the existing JSONL logger format.
hypotheses.deliver:
Ground the implementation in verified cortex knowledge: (1) The always-on daemon lives at ~/.claude/brain/engines/always_on.py — read it before touching it (don't assume the current code matches the S184/S179 descriptions exactly; subsystems drift). (2) Locate the /api/dashboard/brain-tasks endpoint — curl it to confirm response shape before writing the fetch code. (3) Add the fetch call immediately before the cli_invoke / subprocess.run call that spawns claude -p; inject as: '

## ACTIVE BRAIN TASKS
' + json.dumps(tasks, indent=2). (4) For Layer 3, add a tag-check loop: if any task['tags'] contains 'COMS', prepend priority marker to prompt. (5) For Layer 4, append a JSONL record to the existing always-on logger path after each task completes. (6) Do not change the model tier — daemon stays on Sonnet per S190. (7) Verify the first enriched iteration fires correctly before declaring any layer done (R371 — enumerate all gates before declaring fix complete).

hypotheses.label: Contrarian
hypotheses.deconstruct: What if the user is not asking for all four layers to be implemented now, but is instead specifying the complete desired end-state architecture so that Kha'an can self-sequence the work across autonomous iterations? The phrase 'Layer 2 ingest only 1 task at a time' could be a rate-limit instruction for the daemon's operational behavior rather than a build-priority signal. The 'read COMS Visual and add tasks to Brain Visual' in Layer 1 might be the only layer that requires interactive session work — the rest (Layers 2–4) could be daemon-level work that Kha'an executes autonomously.
hypotheses.diagnose: If this reading is correct, the user's primary ask is architectural alignment (understand the full design), not immediate full implementation. The risk of the non-contrarian reads is over-committing to simultaneous multi-layer builds that each touch the same file (always_on.py), creating merge conflicts or half-baked states. The 'report straight back to COMS' option in Layer 4 also hints that the COMS system may already have a receive channel — meaning Layer 4 could be near-zero work if the channel exists.
hypotheses.develop: Adversarial heuristic: assume the user wants Kha'an to act as architect-and-executor but sequence the layers, not parallelize them. The safe path is: (1) confirm COMS Visual read source today (interactive session), (2) implement Layer 2 in the next autonomous daemon cycle, (3) Layer 3 immediately after Layer 2 validates, (4) Layer 4 as the final pass. This avoids the 'sloppy intern' failure pattern (E090) of shipping multiple half-verified changes simultaneously.
hypotheses.deliver: Reframe the delivery: treat this message as an architecture brief, not a sprint ticket. Produce a sequenced execution plan: Phase A (this session) — discover COMS Visual endpoint + Brain Visual write API, confirm /api/dashboard/brain-tasks schema, identify existing COMS report channel. Phase B (next autonomous cycle) — implement Layer 2 fetch-inject in always_on.py, verify via log. Phase C — implement Layer 3 tag-aware branching on top of confirmed Layer 2. Phase D — implement Layer 1 COMS→Brain task sync after Layers 2–3 are stable. Phase E — implement Layer 4 telemetry pointing to the confirmed COMS report channel. Each phase is a single commit. Deliver Phase A findings now; defer code to autonomous cycles.

hypotheses.label: Minimal-action
hypotheses.deconstruct: The smallest action that satisfies this request is implementing Layer 2 alone: add one HTTP fetch of /api/dashboard/brain-tasks to always_on.py and inject the result into the claude -p seed prompt. This single change delivers the most structural value (richer autonomous context per iteration) and is entirely self-contained within one file. Layer 1 (COMS read-write), Layer 3 (tag branching), and Layer 4 (telemetry) all depend on Layer 2 being operational — making Layer 2 the critical path minimum.
hypotheses.diagnose: Risk of minimal-action read: Layer 4 telemetry is described by the identity situational as 'the evidence' that makes work visible to Justs. Shipping Layer 2 without any logging means the work is invisible — which in Justs' results-only frame is equivalent to not having done it. Minimum viable delivery therefore must include at least a stub of Layer 4 (one JSONL line per iteration) to satisfy the observability requirement.
hypotheses.develop: Request type: Technical — Occam's-razor scoping. Minimum viable change: (1) add fetch of /api/dashboard/brain-tasks before claude -p call in always_on.py; (2) inject as a labeled block in the seed prompt; (3) append one JSONL telemetry line per iteration to existing logger. Three additions to one file. No new services, no new endpoints, no schema changes. Layers 1 and 3 are explicitly deferred pending COMS endpoint confirmation.
hypotheses.deliver:
Minimum viable implementation in always_on.py: import requests at the top (if not present). Before the subprocess call that runs claude -p, add: tasks = requests.get('https://goodhealth.lv/api/dashboard/brain-tasks', timeout=5).json() — wrap in try/except so a fetch failure never kills the daemon loop. Append to the seed string: '

## ACTIVE BRAIN TASKS (source: Brain dashboard)
' + json.dumps(tasks[:10], indent=2) (cap at 10 to stay within Sonnet context budget). After the subprocess completes, append one record to the existing JSONL logger: {ts, task_count, model, outcome}. Commit, push, verify one log line. Stop there — Layers 1, 3, 4-full are follow-on work.
